title: Economy: Traders cut rate rise bets after jobs shock
full_text:
Bond markets are no longer confident the Reserve Bank of Australia will lift interest rates further after a surprise jump in the unemployment rate to the highest in more than four years hinted the economy may be slowing down.
The Australian dollar slipped 0.3 per cent to US71.26¢, and the three-year government bond yield, which reflects interest rate expectations, lost 7 basis points to 4.58 per cent. The 10-year bond dropped 5 basis points to 4.96 per cent.
Money markets imply a tiny chance of a rate increase in June, while traders ascribe a 92 per cent chance of a move by Christmas. Before the jobs figures were released, traders had fully priced in an increase to 4.6 per cent, with the decent chance of a subsequent rise.
The central bank has already lifted rates three times this year to head off resurging inflation and offset the impact of a 50 per cent spike in oil prices since the war in Iran erupted nearly three months ago.
However, the outlook is shifting. "The combination of a less-than-expected expansionary federal budget, RBA governor Michele Bullock's seeming desire to keep rates on hold and today's labour market data call into question the need for further rate hikes," said Kris Bernie, a portfolio manager at Kapstream Capital.
He added that global energy pressures would play a big role in future moves. "Any permanent reopening of the Strait of Hormuz would further cement the RBA being on hold for some time," Bernie said.
Societe Generale interest rate strategist Stephen Spratt stressed that while this is the first hard data to soften, leading indicators point to an economic slowdown. "There will be more weakness to come," he warned.
At the same time, federal budget changes could do some of the central bank's heavy lifting. UBS rates strategist Nic Guesnon stressed that changes to negative gearing and taxes could see credit growth - a powerful economic engine in Australia - fall from 8 per cent to 2 per cent. He
The strategist warned that if new lending flows contract by 30 per cent, it will trigger a massive tightening in financial conditions. While some economists argue the tax changes equal one rate rise, he believes the impact could be far larger.
"This could be equivalent to hundreds of basis points of rate hikes," he said.
This is because a slowdown would halt the property wealth effect. If asset growth drops and borrowing stalls, banks and the real estate sector would slow sharply. Guesnon cautioned that this squeeze could become more powerful than the rate hikes already imposed.
While employment data could bounce back, Guesnon argued that continued weakness would force investors to rethink further tightening.
But not everyone is convinced the tightening cycle is over. Fortlake Asset Management founder Christian Baylis expects the economy to weaken, but cautioned that inflation will stay sticky with oil prices above $US100 a barrel, despite a weakening jobs market. Core inflation, the RBA's preferred measure, rose to 3.5 per cent in the first quarter, above the central bank's 2.5 per cent target.
The fund manager believes the central bank will lift the cash rate at least one more time in the second half of the year. "The focus still needs to be on inflation. That's the core problem that the RBA has to solve," he said.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Cecile Lefort
heading: Banking and Economy
